summary: Fowler argues that commercial integration tools (ESBs, ETL, API platforms) are essentially programming languages, not solutions you can purchase off-the-shelf. Organizations mistakenly treat integration as a tactical concern of wiring systems together, when it should be a strategic focus on building clean interfaces over digital capabilities. Low-code integration DSLs suffer from programming-over-time limitations—poor diffing, testing friction, scaling challenges, and operational observability gaps. While these tools can help with workflow simplification and B2B integration long tails, they should never own the interface evolution. Integration strategy is the key architectural driver of organizational acceleration and competitive advantage.
key_points: Integration tools are programming languages bundled with runtimes—when you buy them, you're agreeing to build the actual integration yourself in a commercial DSL
The real goal of integration is creating clean interfaces over capabilities, not wiring systems together—abstract the capability, not the system
Low-code platforms have severe programming-over-time limitations: poor diffability, modularity constraints, testing friction, limited ecosystem evolution, and operational observability gaps
Use general purpose languages to manage interface evolution; use integration DSLs only for bounded implementation concerns like workflow simplification or B2B adapter patterns
Integration strategy lives in the 'second derivative'—it's the key driver of organizational acceleration, not a tactical nuisance to overcome
money_quotes: You can buy programming languages. You can't buy integration.
When you buy an integration product, you are agreeing to build the integration itself in a commercial programming language.
You don't need an SAP API, because your API users don't care about SAP, but you might need an order management API. Abstract the capability, not the system.
The key to simplifying the interface is to accept a more complex implementation.
The lines between applications become the critical cost driver over time.
The role of the architect is to ensure that you don't let a vendor's product strategy become your architectural strategy.
Integration tools 'think' in terms of implementation.
You can't buy integration, but that's OK; it's worth the investment to build it yourself. After all, it may be the most strategic software in your portfolio.
key_insight: Integration is not a tactical problem to be solved with purchased tooling but a strategic capability that requires building clean interfaces over digital capabilities using general-purpose programming languages.
